question: Explain the ACID properties of a transaction in a DBMS.
expectedPoints: Atomicity: Ensures that a transaction is treated as a single, indivisible unit of work. All operations must succeed or the entire transaction fails and is rolled back.
Consistency: Guarantees that a transaction brings the database from one valid state to another, preserving all defined rules and constraints.
Isolation: Ensures that the operations of a concurrent transaction are hidden from other transactions until it is committed, preventing interference.
Durability: Once a transaction is committed, its changes are permanent and persist even in the event of a system failure.

question: Define a schedule in the context of transaction processing and explain what a serial schedule is.
expectedPoints: A schedule is a chronological sequence of the operations from a set of concurrent transactions.
It represents the actual order in which the operations (read, write, commit, abort) are executed by the system.
A serial schedule is one where the operations of each transaction are executed consecutively, without any interleaving of operations from other transactions.
Serial schedules are always correct as they maintain consistency, but they are inefficient because they do not allow for concurrency.

question: Describe the states a transaction can pass through during its execution. Then, explain the specific purpose and function of the database buffer (cache) in this lifecycle, detailing how a DBMS ensures durability despite data initially being written only to this buffer.
expectedPoints: Transaction States: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Failed, and Terminated.
The database buffer is a temporary storage area in main memory that holds data pages read from disk and modified data pages that are waiting to be written back to disk.
When a transaction commits, its changes are written to the buffer, not immediately to disk, for performance reasons.

To ensure durability, the DBMS uses a Write-Ahead Log (WAL) protocol. Before any data page is written to the buffer, the log records (including redo information) describing the change are forced to a stable, non-volatile storage (the log disk).
In the event of a crash, the system can use this durable log to reapply (redo) all committed transactions whose changes may not have yet been written from the buffer to the database files on disk.
